Task instruction sent to agent 
# Implement Buffer Pool Manager

**Repository:** bustub (TheAgentCompany GitLab)  
**Difficulty:** HARD  
**Category:** ccb_tac  
**Task Type:** Systems Programming

## Description

Implement a buffer pool manager for the bustub database system. This is a core component
that manages the in-memory page cache and coordinates with the disk manager for persistence.

**Why this benefits from MCP:**
- Buffer pool manager interacts with multiple components (disk manager, page guard, LRU-K replacer)
- Understanding the dependency graph between components is critical
- Finding how existing managers are implemented provides patterns
- Cross-file relationships and interfaces require deep codebase navigation

## Task

Clone the bustub repository from GitLab (http://the-agent-company.com:8929/root/bustub) 
into the workspace directory. 

You need to write code to fix the issue titled 'Implement Buffer Pool Manager'
(http://the-agent-company.com:8929/root/bustub/-/issues/760). 

You can also find further hints and instructions in the files of the cloned repository.

## Success Criteria

[x] Repository cloned successfully  
[x] Buffer pool manager implementation complete  
[x] All related unit tests pass  
[x] Integration with existing components verified

## Notes

- This is a complex systems programming task
- Understanding the existing architecture is crucial for success
- Reference the issue and README for implementation requirements

     1→<img src="logo/bustub-whiteborder.svg" alt="BusTub Logo" height="200">
     2→
     3→-----------------
     4→
     5→[![Build Status](https://github.com/cmu-db/bustub/actions/workflows/cmake.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/cmu-db/bustub/actions/workflows/cmake.yml)
     6→
     7→BusTub is a relational database management system built at [Carnegie Mellon University](https://db.cs.cmu.edu) for the [Introduction to Database Systems](https://15445.courses.cs.cmu.edu) (15-445/645) course. This system was developed for educational purposes and should not be used in production environments.
     8→
     9→BusTub supports basic SQL and comes with an interactive shell. You can get it running after finishing all the course projects.
    10→
    11→<img src="logo/sql.png" alt="BusTub SQL" width="400">
    12→
    13→**WARNING: IF YOU ARE A STUDENT IN THE CLASS, DO NOT DIRECTLY FORK THIS REPO. DO NOT PUSH PROJECT SOLUTIONS PUBLICLY. THIS IS AN ACADEMIC INTEGRITY VIOLATION AND CAN LEAD TO GETTING YOUR DEGREE REVOKED, EVEN AFTER YOU GRADUATE.**
    14→
    15→We make the autograder for each assignment available to non-CMU students on Gradescope after their due date for CMU students. In exchange for making this available to the public, we ask that you do not make your project implementations public on GitHub or other source code repositories. Please read the course FAQ on how to use the autograder on Gradescope. Run `python3 gradescope_sign.py` to sign an agreement before submitting to the autograder.
    16→
    17→**WARNING: IF YOU ARE A STUDENT OUTSIDE CMU, DO NOT MAKE YOUR SOLUTION PUBLICLY AVAILABLE, AND DO SUBMIT YOUR OWN WORK. OTHERWISE, YOU WILL BE BANNED FROM USING THE AUTOGRADER.** Thank you for creating a fair learning environment.

     2→//
     3→//                         BusTub
     4→//
     5→// buffer_pool_manager.cpp
     6→//
     7→// Identification: src/buffer/buffer_pool_manager.cpp
     8→//
     9→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group

    12→
    13→#include "buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h"
    14→#include "buffer/arc_replacer.h"
    15→#include "common/config.h"
    16→#include "common/macros.h"
    17→
    18→namespace bustub {
    19→
    20→/**
    21→ * @brief The constructor for a `FrameHeader` that initializes all fields to default values.
    22→ *
    23→ * See the documentation for `FrameHeader` in "buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h" for more information.
    24→ *
    25→ * @param frame_id The frame ID / index of the frame we are creating a header for.
    26→ */
    27→FrameHeader::FrameHeader(frame_id_t frame_id) : frame_id_(frame_id), data_(BUSTUB_PAGE_SIZE, 0) { Reset(); }
    28→
    29→/**
    30→ * @brief Get a raw const pointer to the frame's data.
    31→ *
    32→ * @return const char* A pointer to immutable data that the frame stores.
    33→ */
    34→auto FrameHeader::GetData() const -> const char * { return data_.data(); }
    35→
    36→/**
    37→ * @brief Get a raw mutable pointer to the frame's data.
    38→ *
    39→ * @return char* A pointer to mutable data that the frame stores.
    40→ */
    41→auto FrameHeader::GetDataMut() -> char * { return data_.data(); }
    42→
    43→/**
    44→ * @brief Resets a `FrameHeader`'s member fields.
    45→ */
    46→void FrameHeader::Reset() {
    47→  std::fill(data_.begin(), data_.end(), 0);
    48→  pin_c
... (truncated)

     1→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
     2→//
     3→//                         BusTub
     4→//
     5→// buffer_pool_manager.h
     6→//
     7→// Identification: src/include/buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h
     8→//
     9→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group
    10→//
    11→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
    12→
    13→#pragma once
    14→
    15→#include <list>
    16→#include <memory>
    17→#include <shared_mutex>
    18→#include <unordered_map>
    19→#include <vector>
    20→
    21→#include "buffer/arc_replacer.h"
    22→#include "common/config.h"
    23→#include "recovery/log_manager.h"
    24→#include "storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h"
    25→#include "storage/page/page.h"
    26→#include "storage/page/page_guard.h"
    27→
    28→namespace bustub {
    29→
    30→class BufferPoolManager;
    31→class ReadPageGuard;
    32→class WritePageGuard;
    33→
    34→/**
    35→ * @brief A helper class for `BufferPoolManager` that manages a frame of memory and related metadata.
    36→ *
    37→ * This class represents headers for frames of memory that the `BufferPoolManager` stores pages of data into. Note that
    38→ * the actual frames of memory are not stored directly inside a `FrameHeader`, rather the `FrameHeader`s store pointer
    39→ * to the frames and are stored separately them.
    40→ *
    41→ * ---
    42→ *
    43→ * Something that may (or may not) be of interest to you is why the field `data_` is stored as a vector that is
    44→ * allocated on the fly instead of as a direct pointer to some pre-allocated chunk of memory.
    45→ *
    46→ * In a traditional production buffer pool manager, all memory that the buffer pool
... (truncated)

     1→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
     2→//
     3→//                         BusTub
     4→//
     5→// page_guard.h
     6→//
     7→// Identification: src/include/storage/page/page_guard.h
     8→//
     9→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group
    10→//
    11→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
    12→
    13→#pragma once
    14→
    15→#include <memory>
    16→
    17→#include "buffer/arc_replacer.h"
    18→#include "buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h"
    19→#include "storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h"
    20→#include "storage/page/page.h"
    21→
    22→namespace bustub {
    23→
    24→class BufferPoolManager;
    25→class FrameHeader;
    26→
    27→/**
    28→ * @brief An RAII object that grants thread-safe read access to a page of data.
    29→ *
    30→ * The _only_ way that the BusTub system should interact with the buffer pool's page data is via page guards. Since
    31→ * `ReadPageGuard` is an RAII object, the system never has to manually lock and unlock a page's latch.
    32→ *
    33→ * With `ReadPageGuard`s, there can be multiple threads that share read access to a page's data. However, the existence
    34→ * of any `ReadPageGuard` on a page implies that no thread can be mutating the page's data.
    35→ */
    36→class ReadPageGuard {
    37→  /** @brief Only the buffer pool manager is allowed to construct a valid `ReadPageGuard.` */
    38→  friend class BufferPoolManager;
    39→
    40→ public:
    41→  /**
    42→   * @brief The default constructor for a `ReadPageGuard`.
    43→   *
    44→   * Note that we do not EVER want use a guard that has only been default constructed. The only reason we even define
    45→   * this def
... (truncated)

    22→
    23→namespace bustub {
    24→
    25→/**
    26→ * @brief Represents a Write or Read request for the DiskManager to execute.
    27→ */
    28→struct DiskRequest {
    29→  /** Flag indicating whether the request is a write or a read. */
    30→  bool is_write_;
    31→
    32→  /**
    33→   *  Pointer to the start of the memory location where a page is either:
    34→   *   1. being read into from disk (on a read).
    35→   *   2. being written out to disk (on a write).
    36→   */
    37→  char *data_;
    38→
    39→  /** ID of the page being read from / written to disk. */
    40→  page_id_t page_id_;
    41→
    42→  /** Callback used to signal to the request issuer when the request has been completed. */
    43→  std::promise<bool> callback_;
    44→};
    45→
    46→/**
    47→ * @brief The DiskScheduler schedules disk read and write operations.
    48→ *
    49→ * A request is scheduled by calling DiskScheduler::Schedule() with an appropriate DiskRequest object. The scheduler
    50→ * maintains a background worker thread that processes the scheduled requests using the dis
... (truncated)

    35→ *
    36→ * If you wish to refer to the original ARC paper, please note that there are
    37→ * two changes in our implementation:
    38→ * 1. When the size of mru_ equals the target size, we don't check
    39→ * the last access as the paper did when deciding which list to evict from.
    40→ * This is fine since the original decision is stated to be arbitrary.
    41→ * 2. Entries that are not evictable are skipped. If all entries from the desired side
    42→ * (mru_ / mfu_) are pinned, we instead try victimize the other side (mfu_ / mru_),

    26→
    27→/** True if logging should be enabled, false otherwise. */

    35→static constexpr int INVALID_TXN_ID = -1;    // invalid transaction id

     1→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
     2→//
     3→//                         BusTub
     4→//
     5→// channel.h
     6→//
     7→// Identification: src/include/common/channel.h
     8→//
     9→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group
    10→//
    11→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
    12→
    13→#pragma once
    14→
    15→#include <condition_variable>  // NOLINT
    16→#include <mutex>               // NOLINT
    17→#include <queue>
    18→#include <utility>
    19→
    20→namespace bustub {
    21→
    22→/**
    23→ * Channels allow for safe sharing of data between threads. This is a multi-producer multi-consumer channel.
    24→ */
    25→template <class T>
    26→class Channel {
    27→ public:
    28→  Channel() = default;
    29→  ~Channel() = default;
    30→
    31→  /**
    32→   * @brief Inserts an element into a shared queue.
    33→   *
    34→   * @param element The element to be inserted.
    35→   */
    36→  void Put(T element) {
    37→    std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lk(m_);
    38→    q_.push(std::move(element));
    39→    lk.unlock();
    40→    cv_.notify_all();
    41→  }
    42→
    43→  /**
    44→   * @brief Gets an element from the shared queue. If the queue is empty, blocks until an element is available.
    45→   */
    46→  auto Get() -> T {
    47→    std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lk(m_);
    48→    cv_.wait(lk, [&]() { return !q_.empty(); });
    49→    T element = std::move(q_.front());
    50→    q_.pop();
    51→    return element;
    52→  }
    53→
    54→ private:
    55→  std::mutex m_;
    56→  std::condition_variable cv_;
    57→  std::queue<T> q_;
    58→};
    59→}  // namespace bustub
    60→
... (truncated)

    11→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
    12→
    13→#pragma once
    14→
    15→#include <atomic>
    16→#include <filesystem>
    17→#include <fstream>
    18→#include <future>  // NOLINT
    19→#include <mutex>   // NOLINT
    20→#include <string>
    21→#include <unordered_map>
    22→#include <vector>
    23→
    24→#include "common/config.h"
    25→#include "common/logger.h"
    26→
    27→namespace bustub {
    28→
    29→/**
    30→ * DiskManager takes care of the allocation and deallocation of pages within a database. It performs the reading and
    31→ * writing of pages to and from disk, providing a logical file layer within the context of a database management system.
    32→ *
    33→ * DiskManager uses lazy allocation, meaning that it only allocates space on disk when it is first accessed. It
    34→ * maintains a mapping of page ids to their corresponding offsets in the database file. When a page is deleted, it is
    35→ * marked as free and can be reused by future allocations.
    36→ */
    37→class DiskManager {
    38→ public:
    39→  explicit DiskManager(const std::filesystem::path &db_file);
    40→
    41→  /** FOR TEST / LEADERBOARD ONLY, used by DiskManagerMemory */
    42→  DiskManager() = default;
    43→
    44→  virtual ~DiskManager() = default;
    45→
    46→  void ShutDown();
    47→
    48→  /**
    49→   * Write a page to the database file.
    5
... (truncated)

     1→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
     2→//
     3→//                         BusTub
     4→//
     5→// macros.h
     6→//
     7→// Identification: src/include/common/macros.h
     8→//
     9→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group
    10→//
    11→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
    12→
    13→#pragma once
    14→
    15→#include <cassert>
    16→#include <exception>
    17→#include <iostream>
    18→#include <sstream>
    19→#include <stdexcept>
    20→
    21→namespace bustub {  // NOLINT
    22→
    23→#define BUSTUB_ASSERT(expr, message) assert((expr) && (message))
    24→
    25→namespace internal {  // NOLINT
    26→
    27→// An internal stream, used to take C++ stream-style parameters for display, and exit the program with `std::abort`.
    28→class LogFatalStream {
    29→ public:
    30→  LogFatalStream(const char *file, int line) : file_(file), line_(line) {}
    31→
    32→  ~LogFatalStream() {
    33→    std::cerr << file_ << ":" << line_ << ": " << log_stream_.str() << std::endl;
    34→    std::abort();
    35→  }
    36→
    37→  template <typename T>
    38→  auto operator<<(const T &val) -> LogFatalStream & {
    39→    log_stream_ << val;
    40→    return *this;
    41→  }
    42→
    43→ private:
    44→  const char *file_;
    45→  int line_;
    46→  std::ostringstream log_stream_;
    47→};
    48→
    49→}  // namespace internal
    50→
    51→// A macro which checks `expr` value and performs assert.
    52→// Different from `BUSTUB_ASSERT`, it takes stream-style parameters.
    53→#define BUSTUB_ASSERT_AND_LOG(expr)                       /*NOLINT*/ \
    54→  if (bool val = (expr); !val) internal::LogFatalStre
... (truncated)

     1→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
     2→//
     3→//                         BusTub
     4→//
     5→// disk_scheduler.cpp
     6→//
     7→// Identification: src/storage/disk/disk_scheduler.cpp
     8→//
     9→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group
    10→//
    11→//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
    12→
    13→#include "storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h"
    14→#include <vector>
    15→#include "common/macros.h"
    16→#include "storage/disk/disk_manager.h"
    17→
    18→namespace bustub {
    19→
    20→DiskScheduler::DiskScheduler(DiskManager *disk_manager) : disk_manager_(disk_manager) {
    21→  UNIMPLEMENTED("TODO(P1): Add implementation.");
    22→  // Spawn the background thread
    23→  background_thread_.emplace([&] { StartWorkerThread(); });
    24→}
    25→
    26→DiskScheduler::~DiskScheduler() {
    27→  // Put a `std::nullopt` in the queue to signal to exit the loop
    28→  request_queue_.Put(std::nullopt);
    29→  if (background_thread_.has_value()) {
    30→    background_thread_->join();
    31→  }
    32→}
    33→
    34→/**
    35→ * TODO(P1): Add implementation
    36→ *
    37→ * @brief Schedules a request for the DiskManager to execute.
    38→ *
    39→ * @param requests The requests to be scheduled.
    40→ */
    41→void DiskScheduler::Schedule(std::vector<DiskRequest> &requests) {}
    42→
    43→/**
    44→ * TODO(P1): Add implementation
    45→ *
    46→ * @brief Background worker thread function that processes scheduled requests.
    47→ *
    48→ * The background thread needs to process requests while the DiskScheduler exists, i.e., this function should not
    49→ * return until ~DiskScheduler() is called
... (truncated)

    24→ * @brief a new ArcReplacer, with lists initialized to be empty and target size to 0
    25→ * @param num_frames the maximum number of frames the ArcReplacer will be required to cache
    26→ */
    27→ArcReplacer::ArcReplacer(size_t num_frames) : replacer_size_(num_frames) {}

    29→/**
    30→ * TODO(P1): Add implementation
    31→ *
    32→ * @brief Performs the Replace operation as described by the writeup
    33→ * that evicts from either mfu_ or mru_ into its corresponding ghost list
    34→ * according to balancing policy.
    35→ *
    36→ * If you wish to refer to the original ARC paper, please note that there are
    37→ * two changes in our implementation:
    38→ * 1. When the size of mru_ equals the target size, we don't check
    39→ * the last access as the paper did when deciding which list to evict from.
    40→ * This is fine since the original decision is stated to be arbitrary.
    41→ * 2. Entries that are not evictable are skipped. If all entries from the desired side
    42→ * (mru_ / mfu_) are pinned, we instead try victimize the other side (mfu_ / mru_),
    43→ * a
... (truncated)
148-assistanttext-
Now I'll implement the ARC replacer. ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) uses four lists: mru_ (most recently used), mfu_ (most frequently used), mru_ghost_ (ghost of recently evicted from mru), and mfu_ghost_ (ghost of recently evicted from mfu).
149-assistanttool_useRead
150-usertool_resultRead

     9→//
    10→// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group

    24→
    25→namespace bustub {
    26→
    27→enum class AccessType { Unknown = 0, Lookup, Scan, Index };

    29→enum class ArcStatus { MRU, MFU, MRU_GHOST, MFU_GHOST };
    30→
    31→// TODO(student): You can modify or remove this struct as you like.
    32→struct FrameStatus {
    33→  page_id_t page_id_;
    34→  frame_id_t frame_id_;

    37→  FrameStatus(page_id_t pid, frame_id_t fid, bool ev, ArcStatus st)
    38→      : page_id_(pid), frame_id_(fid), evictable_(ev), arc_status_(st) {}
    39→};
    40→
    41→/**
    42→ * ArcReplacer implements the ARC replacement policy.
    43→ */
    44→class ArcReplacer {

    47→
    48→  DISALLOW_COPY_AND_MOVE(ArcReplacer);

    51→   * TODO(P1): Add implementation

    58→  void RecordAccess(frame_id_t frame_id, page_id_t page_id, A

    34→auto FrameHeader::GetData() const -> const char * { return data_.data(); }

   321→  auto disk_manager = std::make_shared<DiskManager>(db_fname);
   322→  auto bpm = std::make_shared<BufferPoolManager>(FRAMES, disk_manager.get());

   329→  // A crude way of synchronizing threads, but works for this small case.
   330→  std::atomic<bool> start = false;
   331→
   332→  auto child = std::thread([&]() {
   333→    // Acknowledge that we can begin the test.
   334→    start.store(true);
   335→
   336→    // Attempt to write to page 0.
   337→    const auto guard0 = bpm->WritePage(pid0);
   338→  });
   339→
   340→  // Wait for the other thread to begin before we start the test.

   342→  }
   343→
   344→  // Make the other thread wait for a bit.
   345→  // This mimics the main thread doing some work while holding the write latch on page 0.
   346→  std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(1000));
   347→
   348→  // If your latching mechanism is incorrect, the next line of code will deadlock.
   349→  // Think about what might happen if you hold a certain "all-encompassing" latch for too long...
   350→
   351→  // While holding page 0, take the latch on page 1.
   352→  const auto guard1 = bpm->WritePage(pid1);
   353→
   354→  // Let the child thread have the page 0 since we're done with it.
   355→  guard0.Drop();
   356→
   357→  child.join();

247-assistanttext-
I see. The test is checking for a deadlock where:
1. Main thread holds write lock on page 0
2. Child thread tries to acquire write lock on page 0 (should block on the frame's rwlatch)
3. Main thread tries to acquire write lock on page 1

The issue is that my CheckedWritePage holds the bpm_latch_ while waiting for the frame lock. The child thread holds bpm_latch while trying to get page 0 (waiting on rwlatch), but the main thread can't enter CheckedWritePage to get page 1 because it's waiting for bpm_latch.

I need to refactor to not hold the bpm_latch while waiting on frame locks. Let me fix this:
248-assistanttool_useRead
249-usertool_resultRead

   141→auto BufferPoolManager::DeletePage(page_id_t page_id) -> bool {
   142→  std::scoped_lock lock(*bpm_latch_);
   143→
   144→  // If the page is not in the buffer pool, just deallocate it on disk
   145→  if (page_table_.count(page_id) == 0) {
   146→    disk_scheduler_->DeallocatePage(page_id);
   147→    return true;
   148→  }
   149→
   150→  // Get the frame holding this page
   151→  frame_id_t frame_id = page_table_[page_id];

   153→
   154→  // If the page is pinned, we can't delete it

   156→    return false;
   157→  }
   158→

   160→  page_table_.erase(page_id);
   161→
   162→  // Remove from replacer

   179→ *
   180→ * Page data can _only_ be accessed via page guards. Users of this `BufferPoolManager` are expected to acquire either a
   181→ * `ReadPageGuard` or a `WritePageGuard` depending on the mode in which they would like to access the data, which

   183→ *

250-assistanttext-
I see the issue - in Case 1, when the page is already in the buffer pool, I'm returning the WritePageGuard inside the bpm_latch scope. The WritePageGuard constructor takes the frame's rwlatch (exclusive lock). But if another thread already holds the rwlatch, we'll be blocked while still holding the bpm_latch.

Let me fix this:

   291→/**
   292→ * @brief Acquires an optional read-locked guard over a page of data. The user can specify an `AccessType` if needed.
   293→ *
   294→ * If it is not possible to bring the page of data into memory, this function will return a `std::nullopt`.
   295→ *
   296→ * Page data can _only_ be accessed via page guards. Users of this `BufferPoolManager` are expected to acquire either a
   297→ * `ReadPageGuard` or a `WritePageGuard` depending on the mode in which they would like to access the data, which
   298→ * ensures that any access of data is thread-safe.
   299→ *
   300→ * There can be any number of `ReadPageGuard`s reading the same page of data at a time across different threads.
   301→ * However, all data access must be immutable. If a user wants to mutate the page's data, they must acquire a
   302→ * `WritePageGuard` with `CheckedWritePage` instead.

   305→ *
   306→ * See the implementation details of `CheckedWritePage`.
   307→ *
   308→ * TODO(P1): Add implementation.

   313→ * returns `std::nullopt`; otherwise, returns a `ReadPageGuard` ensuring shared and read-only access to a page's data.
   314→ */

   360→TEST(BufferPoolManagerTest, DISABLED_EvictableTest) {
   361→  // Test if the evictable status of a frame is always correct.
   362→  const size_t rounds = 1000;
   363→  const size_t num_readers = 8;
   364→
   365→  auto disk_manager = std::make_shared<DiskManager>(db_fname);
   366→  // Only allocate one frame of memory to the buffer pool manager.
   367→  auto bpm = std::make_shared<BufferPoolManager>(1, disk_manager.get());
   368→
   369→  for (size_t i = 0; i < rounds; i++) {
   370→    std::mutex mutex;
   371→    std::condition_variable cv;
   372→
   373→    // This signal tells the readers that they can start reading after the main thread has already taken the read latch.
   374→    bool signal = false;
   375→
   376→    // This page will be loaded into the only available frame.
   377→    const auto winner_pid = bpm->NewPage();
   378→    // We will attempt to load this page into the occupied frame, and it should fail every time.
   379→    const auto loser_pid = bpm->NewPage();
   380→
   381→    std::vector<std::thread> readers;
   382→    for (size_t j = 0; j < num_readers; j++) {
   383→      readers.emplace_back([&]() {
   384→        std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex);
   385→
   386→        // Wait until the main thread has taken a read latch on the page.
   387→        while (!signal) {
   388→          cv.wait(lock);
   389→        }
   390→
   391→        // Read the page in shared mode.
   392→        const auto read_guard = bpm->ReadPage(winner_pid);
   393→
   394→        // Since the only frame is pinned, no thread should be able to bring in a new page.
   395→        ASSERT_FALSE(bpm->CheckedReadPage(loser_pid).has_value());
   396→      });
   397→    }
   398→
   399→    std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex);
   400→
   401→

282-assistanttext-
This is a complex multi-threaded test. There's likely a race condition or invalid memory access. Let me look at my Drop implementations more carefully. 

The issue might be that when we Drop() a guard in the page guard, we need to handle the case where there might be multiple readers of the same page, so pin_count might go from 2 to 1, etc.

Looking more carefully, the issue could also be with the ARC replacer logic. Let me trace through what happens:

1. Main thread calls `ReadPage(winner_pid)` - this should set up the page in the BPM
2. Main thread signals readers
3. Main thread drops the guard - decrementing pin_count
4. Multiple readers try to ReadPage(winner_pid) concurrently

Let me check if there's an issue with how I handle the case when multiple readers try to read the same page. The issue might be in my ARC replacer's handling of pages.

Let me check if there's an issue with accessing frames after they might have been reset. Let me add some debugging by running without address sanitizer:

312-assistanttext-
I see the issue. Looking at the test comments:
- The ARC algorithm evicts from the tail (oldest) of the appropriate list
- My current implementation was iterating from the front

Looking at lines 55-58:
```
// Since target size is still 0, mru side should be evicted
ASSERT_EQ(2, arc_replacer.Evict());  // Expects frame 2 (oldest in mru)
ASSERT_EQ(3, arc_replacer.Evict());  // Expects frame 3
ASSERT_EQ(4, arc_replacer.Evict());  // Expects frame 4
```

The test expects eviction from the **tail** (oldest) of the list, not the front. ARC is an LRU-based algorithm, so we should evict the least recently used, which is at the tail of the list.

Let me fix my Evict implementation to evict from the back:
313-assistanttool_useRead
314-usertool_resultRead
     1→// :bustub-keep-private:

    30→ * TODO(P1): Add implementation

    33→ * that evicts from either mfu_ or mru_ into its corresponding ghost list
    34→ * according to balancing policy.
    35→ *
    36→ * If you wish to refer to the original ARC paper, please note that there are
    37→ * two changes in our implementation:
    38→ * 1. When the size of mru_ equals the target size, we don't check
    39→ * the last access as the paper did when deciding which list to evict from.
    40→ * This is fine since the original decision is stated to be arbitrary.
    41→ * 2. Entries that are not evictable are skipped. If all entries from the desired side
    42→ * (mru_ / mfu_) are pinned, we instead try victimize the other side (mfu_ / mru_),
    43→ * a
... (truncated)

328-assistanttext-
Good progress! The first test passes now, but the second test fails at line 144. Let me check the context around line 144:

Looking at the test code around line 144:
```cpp
// Now [][(4,f3), (1,f2)]![(3,f1)][] p=1
// Make some more ghosts by evicting all pages again
ASSERT_EQ(3, arc_replacer.Evict());  // line 144 - expects frame 3 (mfu side evicted since mru size < target)
ASSERT_EQ(2, arc_replacer.Evict());  // line 145
ASSERT_EQ(1, arc_replacer.Evict());  // line 146
```

Looking at the state: `[][(4,f3), (1,f2)]![(3,f1)][] p=1`
- mru is empty
- mfu has (4,f3), (1,f2)  
- And we have (3,f1) in mfu as well based on the notation

Actually I misread - the notation is:
- `[<-mru_ghost-][<-mru-]![-mfu->][->mfu_ghost->]`

So `[][(4,f3), (1,f2)]![(3,f1)][] p=1` means:
- mru_ghost is empty
- mru has (4,f3), (1,f2)
- mfu has (3,f1)
- mfu_ghost is empty
- target size p=1

When p=1 and mru has 2 items, since mru size (2) > p (1), we should evict from mru. The oldest in mru would be frame 4 (at the back), but the test expects frame 3 which is in mfu...

Wait, let me re-read. The comment says after line 142:
```
// Now [][(4,f3), (1,f2)]![(3,f1)][] p=1
```

But looking at the notation more carefully - items closer to ! are more fresh. So in mru, (1,f2) is more fresh than (4,f3). And (3,f1) is in mfu.

The expected eviction order at line 144 is frame 3, but frame 3 is in mfu. The test expects mfu to be evicted first here. Wait, but mru size is 2 and p is 1, so mru should be evicted.

Let me re-read the test more carefully. After line 139-140:
```cpp
arc_replacer.RecordAccess(1, 3);
arc_replacer.SetEvictable(1, true);
// Now [][(4,f3), (1,f2)]![(3,f1)][] p=1
```

So after this:
- RecordAccess(frame_id=1, page_id=3) - This accesses frame 1 with page 3
- Page 3 was in mru_ghost (from
... (truncated)

355-assistanttext-
The buffer pool manager implementation is complete. Here's a summary of what was implemented:

## Summary

I successfully implemented the buffer pool manager for the BusTub database system. This involved implementing four main components:

### 1. DiskScheduler (`src/storage/disk/disk_scheduler.cpp`)
- Implemented `Schedule()` to put disk requests into the request queue
- Implemented `StartWorkerThread()` to process disk requests from the queue, performing reads/writes via the disk manager

### 2. ARC Replacer (`src/buffer/arc_replacer.cpp`)
- Implemented the Adaptive Replacement Cache (ARC) algorithm with four lists:
  - `mru_` - most recently used list
  - `mfu_` - most frequently used list  
  - `mru_ghost_` - ghost list for recently evicted MRU pages
  - `mfu_ghost_` - ghost list for recently evicted MFU pages
- Key methods implemented:
  - `Evict()` - evicts a frame based on ARC policy, considering target size
  - `RecordAccess()` - records page access and manages list transitions
  - `SetEvictable()` - toggles evictable status
  - `Remove()` - removes a frame from the replacer
  - `Size()` - returns count of evictable frames

### 3. Page Guards (`src/storage/page/page_guard.cpp`)
- Implemented `ReadPageGuard` and `WritePageGuard` RAII classes:
  - Constructors acquire shared/exclusive locks on frames
  - Move constructors/assignment operators for proper resource transfer
  - `Drop()` releases locks and decrements pin counts
  - `Flush()` writes dirty pages to disk

### 4. Buffer Pool Manager (`src/buffer/buffer_pool_manager.cpp`)
- Implemented core methods:
  - `NewPage()` - allocates new page IDs atomically
  - `DeletePage()` - removes pages from buffer pool and disk
  - `CheckedReadPage()` / `CheckedWritePage()` - main page access methods that:
    - Return exist
... (truncated)

28. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/bustub/README.md"
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "<img src=\"logo/bustub-whiteborder.svg\" alt=\"BusTub Logo\" height=\"200\">\n\n-----------------\n\n[![Build Status](https://github.com/cmu-db/bustub/actions/workflows/cmake.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/cmu-db/bustub/actions/workflows/cmake.yml)\n\nBusTub is a relational database management system built at [Carnegie Mellon University](https://db.cs.cmu.edu) for the [Introduction to Database Systems](https://15445.courses.cs.cmu.edu) (15-445/645) course. This system was developed for educational purposes and should not be used in production environments.\n\nBusTub supports basic SQL and comes with an interactive shell. You can get it running after finishing all the course projects.\n\n<img src=\"logo/sql.png\" alt=\"BusTub SQL\" width=\"400\">\n\n**WARNING: IF YOU ARE A STUDENT IN THE CLASS, DO NOT DIRECTLY FORK THIS REPO. DO NOT PUSH PROJECT SOLUTIONS PUBLICLY. THIS IS AN ACADEMIC INTEGRITY VIOLATION AND CAN LEAD TO GETTING YOUR DEGREE REVOKED, EVEN AFTER YOU GRADUATE.**\n\nWe make the autograder for each assignment available to non-CMU students on Gradescope after their due date for CMU students. In exchange for making this available to the public, we ask that you do not make your project implementations public on GitHub or other source code repositories. Please read the course FAQ on how to use the autograder on Gradescope. Run `python3 gradescope_sign.py` to sign an agreement before submitting to the autograder.\n\n**WARNING: IF YOU ARE A STUDENT OUTSIDE CMU, DO NOT MAKE YOUR SOLUTION PUBLICLY AVAILABLE, AND DO SUBMIT YOUR OWN WORK. OTHERWISE, YOU WILL BE BANNED FROM USING THE AUTOGRADER.** Thank you for creating a fair learning environment.\n\n## Cloning this Repository\n\nThe following instructions are adapted from the GitHub documentation on [duplicating a repository](https://docs.github.com/en/github/creating-cloning-and-archiving-repositories/creating-a-repository-on-github/duplicating-a-repository). The procedure below walks you through creating a private BusTub repository that you can use for development.\n\n1. [Create a new repository](https://github.com/new) under your account. Pick a name (e.g. `bustub-private`) and select **Private** for the repository visibility level.\n2. On your development machine, create a bare clone of the public BusTub repository:\n   ```console\n   $ git clone --bare https://github.com/cmu-db/bustub.git bustub-public\n   ```\n3. Next, [mirror](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-push#Documentation/git-push.txt---mirror) the public BusTub repository to your own private BusTub repository. Suppose your GitHub name is `student` and your repository name is `bustub-private`. The procedure for mirroring the repository is then:\n   ```console\n   $ cd bustub-public\n   \n   # If you pull / push over HTTPS\n   $ git push https://github.com/student/bustub-private.git master\n\n   # If you pull / push over SSH\n   $ git push git@github.com:student/bustub-private.git master\n   ```\n   This copies everything in the public BusTub repository to your own private repository. You can now delete your local clone of the public repository:\n   ```console\n   $ cd ..\n   $ rm -rf bustub-public\n   ```\n4. Clone your private repository to your development machine:\n   ```console\n   # If you pull / push over HTTPS\n   $ git clone https://github.com/student/bustub-private.git\n\n   # If you pull / push over SSH\n   $ git clone git@github.com:student/bustub-private.git\n   ```\n5. Add the public BusTub repository as a second remote. This allows you to retrieve changes from the CMU-DB repository and merge them with your solution throughout the semester:\n   ```console\n   $ git remote add public https://github.com/cmu-db/bustub.git\n   ```\n   You can verify that the remote was added with the following command:\n   ```console\n   $ git remote -v\n   origin\thttps://github.com/student/bustub-private.git (fetch)\n   origin\thttps://github.com/student/bustub-private.git (push)\n   public\thttps://github.com/cmu-db/bustub.git (fetch)\n   public\thttps://github.com/cmu-db/bustub.git (push)\n   ```\n6. You can now pull in changes from the public BusTub repository as needed with:\n   ```console\n   $ git pull public master\n   ```\n7. **Disable GitHub Actions** from the project settings of your private repository; otherwise, you may run out of GitHub Actions quota.\n   ```\n   Settings > Actions > General > Actions permissions > Disable actions.\n   ```\n\nWe suggest working on your projects in separate branches. If you do not understand how Git branches work, [learn how](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Branching-Basic-Branching-and-Merging). If you fail to do this, you might lose all your work at some point in the semester, and nobody will be able to help you.\n\n## Build\n\nWe recommend developing BusTub on Ubuntu 24.04, or macOS (M1/M2/Intel). We do not support any other environments (i.e., do not open issues or come to office hours to debug them). We do not support WSL. The grading environment runs\nUbuntu 24.04.\n\n### Linux (Recommended) / macOS (Development Only)\n\nTo ensure that you have the proper packages on your machine, run the following script to automatically install them:\n\n```console\n# Linux\n$ sudo build_support/packages.sh\n# macOS\n$ build_support/packages.sh\n```\n\nThen run the following commands to build the system:\n\n```console\n$ mkdir build\n$ cd build\n$ cmake ..\n$ make\n```\n\nIf you want to compile the system in debug mode, pass in the following flag to cmake:\nDebug mode:\n\n```console\n$ cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug ..\n$ make -j`nproc`\n```\nThis enables [AddressSanitizer](https://github.com/google/sanitizers) by default.\n\nIf you want to use other sanitizers,\n\n```console\n$ cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug -DBUSTUB_SANITIZER=thread ..\n$ make -j`nproc`\n```\n\nThere are some differences between macOS and Linux (i.e., mutex behavior) that might cause test cases\nto produce different results in different platforms. We recommend students to use a Linux VM for running\ntest cases and reproducing errors whenever possible.\n",
    "filePath": "/workspace/bustub/README.md",
    "numLines": 118,
    "startLine": 1,
    "totalLines": 118
  },
  "type": "text"
}

    "content": "//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n//\n//                         BusTub\n//\n// buffer_pool_manager.cpp\n//\n// Identification: src/buffer/buffer_pool_manager.cpp\n//\n// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group\n//\n//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n\n#include \"buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h\"\n#include \"buffer/arc_replacer.h\"\n#include \"common/config.h\"\n#include \"common/macros.h\"\n\nnamespace bustub {\n\n/**\n * @brief The constructor for a `FrameHeader` that initializes all fields to default values.\n *\n * See the documentation for `FrameHeader` in \"buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h\" for more information.\n *\n * @param frame_id The frame ID / index of the frame we are creating a header for.\n */\nFrameHeader::FrameHeader(frame_id_t frame_id) : frame_id_(frame_id), data_(BUSTUB_PAGE_SIZE, 0) { Reset(); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Get a raw const pointer to the frame's data.\n *\n * @return const char* A pointer to immutable data that the frame stores.\n */\nauto FrameHeader::GetData() const -> const char * { return data_.data(); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Get a raw mutable pointer to the frame's data.\n *\n * @return char* A pointer to mutable data that the frame stores.\n */\nauto FrameHeader::GetDataMut() -> char * { return data_.data(); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Resets a `FrameHeader`'s member fields.\n */\nvoid FrameHeader::Reset() {\n  std::fill(data_.begin(), data_.end(), 0);\n  pin_count_.store(0);\n  is_dirty_ = false;\n}\n\n/**\n * @brief Creates a new `BufferPoolManager` instance and initializes all fields.\n *\n * See the documentation for `BufferPoolManager` in \"buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h\" for more information.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * We have implemented the constructor for you in a way that makes sense with our reference solution. You are free to\n * change anything you would like here if it doesn't fit with you implementation.\n *\n * Be warned, though! If you stray too far away from our guidance, it will be much harder for us to help you. Our\n * recommendation would be to first implement the buffer pool manager using the stepping stones we have provided.\n *\n * Once you have a fully working solution (all Gradescope test cases pass), then you can try more interesting things!\n *\n * @param num_frames The size of the buffer pool.\n * @param disk_manager The disk manager.\n * @param log_manager The log manager. Please ignore this for P1.\n */\nBufferPoolManager::BufferPoolManager(size_t num_frames, DiskManager *disk_manager, LogManager *log_manager)\n    : num_frames_(num_frames),\n      next_page_id_(0),\n      bpm_latch_(std::make_shared<std::mutex>()),\n      replacer_(std::make_shared<ArcReplacer>(num_frames)),\n      disk_scheduler_(std::make_shared<DiskScheduler>(disk_manager)),\n      log_manager_(log_manager) {\n  // Not strictly necessary...\n  std::scoped_lock latch(*bpm_latch_);\n\n  // Initialize the monotonically increasing counter at 0.\n  next_page_id_.store(0);\n\n  // Allocate all of the in-memory frames up front.\n  frames_.reserve(num_frames_);\n\n  // The page table should have exactly `num_frames_` slots, corresponding to exactly `num_frames_` frames.\n  page_table_.reserve(num_frames_);\n\n  // Initialize all of the frame headers, and fill the free frame list with all possible frame IDs (since all frames are\n  // initially free).\n  for (size_t i = 0; i < num_frames_; i++) {\n    frames_.push_back(std::make_shared<FrameHeader>(i));\n    free_frames_.push_back(static_cast<int>(i));\n  }\n}\n\n/**\n * @brief Destroys the `BufferPoolManager`, freeing up all memory that the buffer pool was using.\n */\nBufferPoolManager::~BufferPoolManager() = default;\n\n/**\n * @brief Returns the number of frames that this buffer pool manages.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::Size() const -> size_t { return num_frames_; }\n\n/**\n * @brief Allocates a new page on disk.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * You will maintain a thread-safe, monotonically increasing counter in the form of a `std::atomic<page_id_t>`.\n * See the documentation on [atomics](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/atomic/atomic) for more information.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation.\n *\n * @return The page ID of the newly allocated page.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::NewPage() -> page_id_t { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Removes a page from the database, both on disk and in memory.\n *\n * If the page is pinned in the buffer pool, this function does nothing and returns `false`. Otherwise, this function\n * removes the page from both disk and memory (if it is still in the buffer pool), returning `true`.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * Think about all of the places that a page or a page's metadata could be, and use that to guide you on implementing\n * this function. You will probably want to implement this function _after_ you have implemented `CheckedReadPage` and\n * `CheckedWritePage`.\n *\n * You should call `DeallocatePage` in the disk scheduler to make the space available for new pages.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation.\n *\n * @param page_id The page ID of the page we want to delete.\n * @return `false` if the page exists but could not be deleted, `true` if the page didn't exist or deletion succeeded.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::DeletePage(page_id_t page_id) -> bool { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Acquires an optional write-locked guard over a page of data. The user can specify an `AccessType` if needed.\n *\n * If it is not possible to bring the page of data into memory, this function will return a `std::nullopt`.\n *\n * Page data can _only_ be accessed via page guards. Users of this `BufferPoolManager` are expected to acquire either a\n * `ReadPageGuard` or a `WritePageGuard` depending on the mode in which they would like to access the data, which\n * ensures that any access of data is thread-safe.\n *\n * There can only be 1 `WritePageGuard` reading/writing a page at a time. This allows data access to be both immutable\n * and mutable, meaning the thread that owns the `WritePageGuard` is allowed to manipulate the page's data however they\n * want. If a user wants to have multiple threads reading the page at the same time, they must acquire a `ReadPageGuard`\n * with `CheckedReadPage` instead.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * There are three main cases that you will have to implement. The first two are relatively simple: one is when there is\n * plenty of available memory, and the other is when we don't actually need to perform any additional I/O. Think about\n * what exactly these two cases entail.\n *\n * The third case is the trickiest, and it is when we do not have any _easily_ available memory at our disposal. The\n * buffer pool is tasked with finding memory that it can use to bring in a page of memory, using the replacement\n * algorithm you implemented previously to find candidate frames for eviction.\n *\n * Once the buffer pool has identified a frame for eviction, several I/O operations may be necessary to bring in the\n * page of data we want into the frame.\n *\n * There is likely going to be a lot of shared code with `CheckedReadPage`, so you may find creating helper functions\n * useful.\n *\n * These two functions are the crux of this project, so we won't give you more hints than this. Good luck!\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation.\n *\n * @param page_id The ID of the page we want to write to.\n * @param access_type The type of page access.\n * @return std::optional<WritePageGuard> An optional latch guard where if there are no more free frames (out of memory)\n * returns `std::nullopt`; otherwise, returns a `WritePageGuard` ensuring exclusive and mutable access to a page's data.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::CheckedWritePage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type) -> std::optional<WritePageGuard> {\n  UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\");\n}\n\n/**\n * @brief Acquires an optional read-locked guard over a page of data. The user can specify an `AccessType` if needed.\n *\n * If it is not possible to bring the page of data into memory, this function will return a `std::nullopt`.\n *\n * Page data can _only_ be accessed via page guards. Users of this `BufferPoolManager` are expected to acquire either a\n * `ReadPageGuard` or a `WritePageGuard` depending on the mode in which they would like to access the data, which\n * ensures that any access of data is thread-safe.\n *\n * There can be any number of `ReadPageGuard`s reading the same page of data at a time across different threads.\n * However, all data access must be immutable. If a user wants to mutate the page's data, they must acquire a\n * `WritePageGuard` with `CheckedWritePage` instead.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * See the implementation details of `CheckedWritePage`.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation.\n *\n * @param page_id The ID of the page we want to read.\n * @param access_type The type of page access.\n * @return std::optional<ReadPageGuard> An optional latch guard where if there are no more free frames (out of memory)\n * returns `std::nullopt`; otherwise, returns a `ReadPageGuard` ensuring shared and read-only access to a page's data.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::CheckedReadPage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type) -> std::optional<ReadPageGuard> {\n  UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\");\n}\n\n/**\n * @brief A wrapper around `CheckedWritePage` that unwraps the inner value if it exists.\n *\n * If `CheckedWritePage` returns a `std::nullopt`, **this function aborts the entire process.**\n *\n * This function should **only** be used for testing and ergonomic's sake. If it is at all possible that the buffer pool\n * manager might run out of memory, then use `CheckedPageWrite` to allow you to handle that case.\n *\n * See the documentation for `CheckedPageWrite` for more information about implementation.\n *\n * @param page_id The ID of the page we want to read.\n * @param access_type The type of page access.\n * @return WritePageGuard A page guard ensuring exclusive and mutable access to a page's data.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::WritePage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type) -> WritePageGuard {\n  auto guard_opt = CheckedWritePage(page_id, access_type);\n\n  if (!guard_opt.has_value()) {\n    fmt::println(stderr, \"\\n`CheckedWritePage` failed to bring in page {}\\n\", page_id);\n    std::abort();\n  }\n\n  return std::move(guard_opt).value();\n}\n\n/**\n * @brief A wrapper around `CheckedReadPage` that unwraps the inner value if it exists.\n *\n * If `CheckedReadPage` returns a `std::nullopt`, **this function aborts the entire process.**\n *\n * This function should **only** be used for testing and ergonomic's sake. If it is at all possible that the buffer pool\n * manager might run out of memory, then use `CheckedPageWrite` to allow you to handle that case.\n *\n * See the documentation for `CheckedPageRead` for more information about implementation.\n *\n * @param page_id The ID of the page we want to read.\n * @param access_type The type of page access.\n * @return ReadPageGuard A page guard ensuring shared and read-only access to a page's data.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::ReadPage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type) -> ReadPageGuard {\n  auto guard_opt = CheckedReadPage(page_id, access_type);\n\n  if (!guard_opt.has_value()) {\n    fmt::println(stderr, \"\\n`CheckedReadPage` failed to bring in page {}\\n\", page_id);\n    std::abort();\n  }\n\n  return std::move(guard_opt).value();\n}\n\n/**\n * @brief Flushes a page's data out to disk unsafely.\n *\n * This function will write out a page's data to disk if it has been modified. If the given page is not in memory, this\n * function will return `false`.\n *\n * You should not take a lock on the page in this function.\n * This means that you should carefully consider when to toggle the `is_dirty_` bit.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * You should probably leave implementing this function until after you have completed `CheckedReadPage` and\n * `CheckedWritePage`, as it will likely be much easier to understand what to do.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation\n *\n * @param page_id The page ID of the page to be flushed.\n * @return `false` if the page could not be found in the page table; otherwise, `true`.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::FlushPageUnsafe(page_id_t page_id) -> bool { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Flushes a page's data out to disk safely.\n *\n * This function will write out a page's data to disk if it has been modified. If the given page is not in memory, this\n * function will return `false`.\n *\n * You should take a lock on the page in this function to ensure that a consistent state is flushed to disk.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * You should probably leave implementing this function until after you have completed `CheckedReadPage`,\n * `CheckedWritePage`, and `Flush` in the page guards, as it will likely be much easier to understand what to do.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation\n *\n * @param page_id The page ID of the page to be flushed.\n * @return `false` if the page could not be found in the page table; otherwise, `true`.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::FlushPage(page_id_t page_id) -> bool { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Flushes all page data that is in memory to disk unsafely.\n *\n * You should not take locks on the pages in this function.\n * This means that you should carefully consider when to toggle the `is_dirty_` bit.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * You should probably leave implementing this function until after you have completed `CheckedReadPage`,\n * `CheckedWritePage`, and `FlushPage`, as it will likely be much easier to understand what to do.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation\n */\nvoid BufferPoolManager::FlushAllPagesUnsafe() { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Flushes all page data that is in memory to disk safely.\n *\n * You should take locks on the pages in this function to ensure that a consistent state is flushed to disk.\n *\n * ### Implementation\n *\n * You should probably leave implementing this function until after you have completed `CheckedReadPage`,\n * `CheckedWritePage`, and `FlushPage`, as it will likely be much easier to understand what to do.\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation\n */\nvoid BufferPoolManager::FlushAllPages() { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/**\n * @brief Retrieves the pin count of a page. If the page does not exist in memory, return `std::nullopt`.\n *\n * This function is thread safe. Callers may invoke this function in a multi-threaded environment where multiple threads\n * access the same page.\n *\n * This function is intended for testing purposes. If this function is implemented incorrectly, it will definitely cause\n * problems with the test suite and autograder.\n *\n * # Implementation\n *\n * We will use this function to test if your buffer pool manager is managing pin counts correctly. Since the\n * `pin_count_` field in `FrameHeader` is an atomic type, you do not need to take the latch on the frame that holds the\n * page we want to look at. Instead, you can simply use an atomic `load` to safely load the value stored. You will still\n * need to take the buffer pool latch, however.\n *\n * Again, if you are unfamiliar with atomic types, see the official C++ docs\n * [here](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/atomic/atomic).\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation\n *\n * @param page_id The page ID of the page we want to get the pin count of.\n * @return std::optional<size_t> The pin count if the page exists; otherwise, `std::nullopt`.\n */\nauto BufferPoolManager::GetPinCount(page_id_t page_id) -> std::optional<size_t> {\n  UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\");\n}\n\n}  // namespace bustub\n",

32. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/bustub/src/include/buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h"
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n//\n//                         BusTub\n//\n// buffer_pool_manager.h\n//\n// Identification: src/include/buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h\n//\n// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group\n//\n//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n\n#pragma once\n\n#include <list>\n#include <memory>\n#include <shared_mutex>\n#include <unordered_map>\n#include <vector>\n\n#include \"buffer/arc_replacer.h\"\n#include \"common/config.h\"\n#include \"recovery/log_manager.h\"\n#include \"storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h\"\n#include \"storage/page/page.h\"\n#include \"storage/page/page_guard.h\"\n\nnamespace bustub {\n\nclass BufferPoolManager;\nclass ReadPageGuard;\nclass WritePageGuard;\n\n/**\n * @brief A helper class for `BufferPoolManager` that manages a frame of memory and related metadata.\n *\n * This class represents headers for frames of memory that the `BufferPoolManager` stores pages of data into. Note that\n * the actual frames of memory are not stored directly inside a `FrameHeader`, rather the `FrameHeader`s store pointer\n * to the frames and are stored separately them.\n *\n * ---\n *\n * Something that may (or may not) be of interest to you is why the field `data_` is stored as a vector that is\n * allocated on the fly instead of as a direct pointer to some pre-allocated chunk of memory.\n *\n * In a traditional production buffer pool manager, all memory that the buffer pool is intended to manage is allocated\n * in one large contiguous array (think of a very large `malloc` call that allocates several gigabytes of memory up\n * front). This large contiguous block of memory is then divided into contiguous frames. In other words, frames are\n * defined by an offset from the base of the array in page-sized (4 KB) intervals.\n *\n * In BusTub, we instead allocate each frame on its own (via a `std::vector<char>`) in order to easily detect buffer\n * overflow with address sanitizer. Since C++ has no notion of memory safety, it would be very easy to cast a page's\n * data pointer into some large data type and start overwriting other pages of data if they were all contiguous.\n *\n * If you would like to attempt to use more efficient data structures for your buffer pool manager, you are free to do\n * so. However, you will likely benefit significantly from detecting buffer overflow in future projects (especially\n * project 2).\n */\nclass FrameHeader {\n  friend class BufferPoolManager;\n  friend class ReadPageGuard;\n  friend class WritePageGuard;\n\n public:\n  explicit FrameHeader(frame_id_t frame_id);\n\n private:\n  auto GetData() const -> const char *;\n  auto GetDataMut() -> char *;\n  void Reset();\n\n  /** @brief The frame ID / index of the frame this header represents. */\n  const frame_id_t frame_id_;\n\n  /** @brief The readers / writer latch for this frame. */\n  std::shared_mutex rwlatch_;\n\n  /** @brief The number of pins on this frame keeping the page in memory. */\n  std::atomic<size_t> pin_count_;\n\n  /** @brief The dirty flag. */\n  bool is_dirty_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A pointer to the data of the page that this frame holds.\n   *\n   * If the frame does not hold any page data, the frame contains all null bytes.\n   */\n  std::vector<char> data_;\n\n  /**\n   * TODO(P1): You may add any fields or helper functions under here that you think are necessary.\n   *\n   * One potential optimization you could make is storing an optional page ID of the page that the `FrameHeader` is\n   * currently storing. This might allow you to skip searching for the corresponding (page ID, frame ID) pair somewhere\n   * else in the buffer pool manager...\n   */\n};\n\n/**\n * @brief The declaration of the `BufferPoolManager` class.\n *\n * As stated in the writeup, the buffer pool is responsible for moving physical pages of data back and forth from\n * buffers in main memory to persistent storage. It also behaves as a cache, keeping frequently used pages in memory for\n * faster access, and evicting unused or cold pages back out to storage.\n *\n * Make sure you read the writeup in its entirety before attempting to implement the buffer pool manager. You also need\n * to have completed the implementation of both the `ArcReplacer` and `DiskManager` classes.\n */\nclass BufferPoolManager {\n public:\n  BufferPoolManager(size_t num_frames, DiskManager *disk_manager, LogManager *log_manager = nullptr);\n  ~BufferPoolManager();\n\n  auto Size() const -> size_t;\n  auto NewPage() -> page_id_t;\n  auto DeletePage(page_id_t page_id) -> bool;\n  auto CheckedWritePage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type = AccessType::Unknown)\n      -> std::optional<WritePageGuard>;\n  auto CheckedReadPage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type = AccessType::Unknown) -> std::optional<ReadPageGuard>;\n  auto WritePage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type = AccessType::Unknown) -> WritePageGuard;\n  auto ReadPage(page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type = AccessType::Unknown) -> ReadPageGuard;\n  auto FlushPageUnsafe(page_id_t page_id) -> bool;\n  auto FlushPage(page_id_t page_id) -> bool;\n  void FlushAllPagesUnsafe();\n  void FlushAllPages();\n  auto GetPinCount(page_id_t page_id) -> std::optional<size_t>;\n\n private:\n  /** @brief The number of frames in the buffer pool. */\n  const size_t num_frames_;\n\n  /** @brief The next page ID to be allocated.  */\n  std::atomic<page_id_t> next_page_id_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief The latch protecting the buffer pool's inner data structures.\n   *\n   * TODO(P1) We recommend replacing this comment with details about what this latch actually protects.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<std::mutex> bpm_latch_;\n\n  /** @brief The frame headers of the frames that this buffer pool manages. */\n  std::vector<std::shared_ptr<FrameHeader>> frames_;\n\n  /** @brief The page table that keeps track of the mapping between pages and buffer pool frames. */\n  std::unordered_map<page_id_t, frame_id_t> page_table_;\n\n  /** @brief A list of free frames that do not hold any page's data. */\n  std::list<frame_id_t> free_frames_;\n\n  /** @brief The replacer to find unpinned / candidate pages for eviction. */\n  std::shared_ptr<ArcReplacer> replacer_;\n\n  /** @brief A pointer to the disk scheduler. Shared with the page guards for flushing. */\n  std::shared_ptr<DiskScheduler> disk_scheduler_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A pointer to the log manager.\n   *\n   * Note: Please ignore this for P1.\n   */\n  LogManager *log_manager_ __attribute__((__unused__));\n\n  /**\n   * TODO(P1): You may add additional private members and helper functions if you find them necessary.\n   *\n   * There will likely be a lot of code duplication between the different modes of accessing a page.\n   *\n   * We would recommend implementing a helper function that returns the ID of a frame that is free and has nothing\n   * stored inside of it. Additionally, you may also want to implement a helper function that returns either a shared\n   * pointer to a `FrameHeader` that already has a page's data stored inside of it, or an index to said `FrameHeader`.\n   */\n};\n}  // namespace bustub\n",

    "content": "//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n//\n//                         BusTub\n//\n// page_guard.h\n//\n// Identification: src/include/storage/page/page_guard.h\n//\n// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group\n//\n//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n\n#pragma once\n\n#include <memory>\n\n#include \"buffer/arc_replacer.h\"\n#include \"buffer/buffer_pool_manager.h\"\n#include \"storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h\"\n#include \"storage/page/page.h\"\n\nnamespace bustub {\n\nclass BufferPoolManager;\nclass FrameHeader;\n\n/**\n * @brief An RAII object that grants thread-safe read access to a page of data.\n *\n * The _only_ way that the BusTub system should interact with the buffer pool's page data is via page guards. Since\n * `ReadPageGuard` is an RAII object, the system never has to manually lock and unlock a page's latch.\n *\n * With `ReadPageGuard`s, there can be multiple threads that share read access to a page's data. However, the existence\n * of any `ReadPageGuard` on a page implies that no thread can be mutating the page's data.\n */\nclass ReadPageGuard {\n  /** @brief Only the buffer pool manager is allowed to construct a valid `ReadPageGuard.` */\n  friend class BufferPoolManager;\n\n public:\n  /**\n   * @brief The default constructor for a `ReadPageGuard`.\n   *\n   * Note that we do not EVER want use a guard that has only been default constructed. The only reason we even define\n   * this default constructor is to enable placing an \"uninitialized\" guard on the stack that we can later move assign\n   * via `=`.\n   *\n   * **Use of an uninitialized page guard is undefined behavior.**\n   *\n   * In other words, the only way to get a valid `ReadPageGuard` is through the buffer pool manager.\n   */\n  ReadPageGuard() = default;\n\n  ReadPageGuard(const ReadPageGuard &) = delete;\n  auto operator=(const ReadPageGuard &) -> ReadPageGuard & = delete;\n  ReadPageGuard(ReadPageGuard &&that) noexcept;\n  auto operator=(ReadPageGuard &&that) noexcept -> ReadPageGuard &;\n  auto GetPageId() const -> page_id_t;\n  auto GetData() const -> const char *;\n  template <class T>\n  auto As() const -> const T * {\n    return reinterpret_cast<const T *>(GetData());\n  }\n  auto IsDirty() const -> bool;\n  void Flush();\n  void Drop();\n  ~ReadPageGuard();\n\n private:\n  /** @brief Only the buffer pool manager is allowed to construct a valid `ReadPageGuard.` */\n  explicit ReadPageGuard(page_id_t page_id, std::shared_ptr<FrameHeader> frame, std::shared_ptr<ArcReplacer> replacer,\n                         std::shared_ptr<std::mutex> bpm_latch, std::shared_ptr<DiskScheduler> disk_scheduler);\n\n  /** @brief The page ID of the page we are guarding. */\n  page_id_t page_id_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief The frame that holds the page this guard is protecting.\n   *\n   * Almost all operations of this page guard should be done via this shared pointer to a `FrameHeader`.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<FrameHeader> frame_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A shared pointer to the buffer pool's replacer.\n   *\n   * Since the buffer pool cannot know when this `ReadPageGuard` gets destructed, we maintain a pointer to the buffer\n   * pool's replacer in order to set the frame as evictable on destruction.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<ArcReplacer> replacer_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A shared pointer to the buffer pool's latch.\n   *\n   * Since the buffer pool cannot know when this `ReadPageGuard` gets destructed, we maintain a pointer to the buffer\n   * pool's latch for when we need to update the frame's eviction state in the buffer pool replacer.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<std::mutex> bpm_latch_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A shared pointer to the buffer pool's disk scheduler.\n   *\n   * Used when flushing pages to disk.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<DiskScheduler> disk_scheduler_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief The validity flag for this `ReadPageGuard`.\n   *\n   * Since we must allow for the construction of invalid page guards (see the documentation above), we must maintain\n   * some sort of state that tells us if this page guard is valid or not. Note that the default constructor will\n   * automatically set this field to `false`.\n   *\n   * If we did not maintain this flag, then the move constructor / move assignment operators could attempt to destruct\n   * or `Drop()` invalid members, causing a segmentation fault.\n   */\n  bool is_valid_{false};\n\n  /**\n   * TODO(P1): You may add any fields under here that you think are necessary.\n   *\n   * If you want extra (nonexistent) style points, and you want to be extra fancy, then you can look into the\n   * `std::shared_lock` type and use that for the latching mechanism instead of manually calling `lock` and `unlock`.\n   */\n};\n\n/**\n * @brief An RAII object that grants thread-safe write access to a page of data.\n *\n * The _only_ way that the BusTub system should interact with the buffer pool's page data is via page guards. Since\n * `WritePageGuard` is an RAII object, the system never has to manually lock and unlock a page's latch.\n *\n * With a `WritePageGuard`, there can be only be one thread that has exclusive ownership over the page's data. This\n * means that the owner of the `WritePageGuard` can mutate the page's data as much as they want. However, the existence\n * of a `WritePageGuard` implies that no other `WritePageGuard` or any `ReadPageGuard`s for the same page can exist at\n * the same time.\n */\nclass WritePageGuard {\n  /** @brief Only the buffer pool manager is allowed to construct a valid `WritePageGuard.` */\n  friend class BufferPoolManager;\n\n public:\n  /**\n   * @brief The default constructor for a `WritePageGuard`.\n   *\n   * Note that we do not EVER want use a guard that has only been default constructed. The only reason we even define\n   * this default constructor is to enable placing an \"uninitialized\" guard on the stack that we can later move assign\n   * via `=`.\n   *\n   * **Use of an uninitialized page guard is undefined behavior.**\n   *\n   * In other words, the only way to get a valid `WritePageGuard` is through the buffer pool manager.\n   */\n  WritePageGuard() = default;\n\n  WritePageGuard(const WritePageGuard &) = delete;\n  auto operator=(const WritePageGuard &) -> WritePageGuard & = delete;\n  WritePageGuard(WritePageGuard &&that) noexcept;\n  auto operator=(WritePageGuard &&that) noexcept -> WritePageGuard &;\n  auto GetPageId() const -> page_id_t;\n  auto GetData() const -> const char *;\n  template <class T>\n  auto As() const -> const T * IsDirty() const -> bool;\n  void Flush();\n  void Drop();\n  ~WritePageGuard();\n\n private:\n  /** @brief Only the buffer pool manager is allowed to construct a valid `WritePageGuard.` */\n  explicit WritePageGuard(page_id_t page_id, std::shared_ptr<FrameHeader> frame, std::shared_ptr<ArcReplacer> replacer,\n                          std::shared_ptr<std::mutex> bpm_latch, std::shared_ptr<DiskScheduler> disk_scheduler);\n\n  /** @brief The page ID of the page we are guarding. */\n  page_id_t page_id_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief The frame that holds the page this guard is protecting.\n   *\n   * Almost all operations of this page guard should be done via this shared pointer to a `FrameHeader`.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<FrameHeader> frame_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A shared pointer to the buffer pool's replacer.\n   *\n   * Since the buffer pool cannot know when this `WritePageGuard` gets destructed, we maintain a pointer to the buffer\n   * pool's replacer in order to set the frame as evictable on destruction.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<ArcReplacer> replacer_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A shared pointer to the buffer pool's latch.\n   *\n   * Since the buffer pool cannot know when this `WritePageGuard` gets destructed, we maintain a pointer to the buffer\n   * pool's latch for when we need to update the frame's eviction state in the buffer pool replacer.\n   */\n  std::shared_ptr<std::mutex> bpm_latch_;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief A shared pointer to the buffer pool's disk scheduler.\n   *\n   * Used when flushing pages to disk.\n   */\n If you get the ordering wrong, you will very likely fail one of the later\n * Gradescope tests. You may also want to take the buffer pool manager's latch in a very specific scenario...\n *\n * TODO(P1): Add implementation.\n */\nvoid ReadPageGuard::Drop() { UNIMPLEMENTED(\"TODO(P1): Add implementation.\"); }\n\n/** @brief The destructor for `ReadPageGuard`. Output

  "file": {
    "content": "\n//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n//\n//                         BusTub\n//\n// arc_replacer.h\n//\n// Identification: src/include/buffer/arc_replacer.h\n//\n// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group\n//\n//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n\n#pragma once\n\n#include <list>\n#include <memory>\n#include <mutex>  // NOLINT\n#include <optional>\n#include <unordered_map>\n\n#include \"common/config.h\"\n#include \"common/macros.h\"\n\nnamespace bustub {\n\nenum class AccessType { Unknown = 0, Lookup, Scan, Index };\n\nenum class ArcStatus { MRU, MFU, MRU_GHOST, MFU_GHOST };\n\n// TODO(student): You can modify or remove this struct as you like.\nstruct FrameStatus {\n  page_id_t page_id_;\n  frame_id_t frame_id_;\n  bool evictable_;\n  ArcStatus arc_status_;\n  FrameStatus(page_id_t pid, frame_id_t fid, bool ev, ArcStatus st)\n      : page_id_(pid), frame_id_(fid), evictable_(ev), arc_status_(st) {}\n};\n\n/**\n * ArcReplacer implements the ARC replacement policy.\n */\nclass ArcReplacer {\n public:\n  explicit ArcReplacer(size_t num_frames);\n\n  DISALLOW_COPY_AND_MOVE(ArcReplacer);\n\n  /**\n   * TODO(P1): Add implementation\n   *\n   * @brief Destroys the LRUReplacer.\n   */\n  ~ArcReplacer() = default;\n\n  auto Evict() -> std::optional<frame_id_t>;\n  void RecordAccess(frame_id_t frame_id, page_id_t page_id, AccessType access_type = AccessType::Unknown);\n  void SetEvictable(frame_id_t frame_id, bool set_evictable);\n  void Remove(frame_id_t frame_id);\n  auto Size() -> size_t;\n\n private:\n  // TODO(student): implement me! You can replace or remove these member variables as you like.\n  std::list<frame_id_t> mru_;\n  std::list<frame_id_t> mfu_;\n  std::list<page_id_t> mru_ghost_;\n  std::list<page_id_t> mfu_ghost_;\n\n  /* record entries in mru_ and mfu_\n   * this uses frame_id_t to guarantee no duplicate records for the same\n   * frame when they are alive */\n  std::unordered_map<frame_id_t, std::shared_ptr<FrameStatus>> alive_map_;\n  /* record entries in mru_ghost_ and mfu_ghost_\n   * this uses page_id_t but not frame_id_t because page_id is the unique\n   * identifier in ghost lists */\n  std::unordered_map<page_id_t, std::shared_ptr<FrameStatus>> ghost_map_;\n\n  /* alive, evictable entries count */\n  [[maybe_unused]] size_t curr_size_{0};\n  /* p as in original paper */\n  [[maybe_unused]] size_t mru_target_size_{0};\n  /* c as in original paper */\n  [[maybe_unused]] size_t replacer_size_;\n  std::mutex latch_;\n\n  // TODO(student): You can add member variables / functions as you like.\n};\n\n}  // namespace bustub\n",
    "filePath": "/workspace/bustub/src/include/buffer/arc_replacer.h",
    "numLines": 91,
    "startLine": 1,
    "totalLines": 91
  },

37. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/bustub/src/include/storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h"
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n//\n//                         BusTub\n//\n// disk_scheduler.h\n//\n// Identification: src/include/storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h\n//\n// Copyright (c) 2015-2025, Carnegie Mellon University Database Group\n//\n//===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//\n\n#pragma once\n\n#include <future>  // NOLINT\n#include <optional>\n#include <thread>  // NOLINT\n#include <vector>\n\n#include \"common/channel.h\"\n#include \"storage/disk/disk_manager.h\"\n\nnamespace bustub {\n\n/**\n * @brief Represents a Write or Read request for the DiskManager to execute.\n */\nstruct DiskRequest {\n  /** Flag indicating whether the request is a write or a read. */\n  bool is_write_;\n\n  /**\n   *  Pointer to the start of the memory location where a page is either:\n   *   1. being read into from disk (on a read).\n   *   2. being written out to disk (on a write).\n   */\n  char *data_;\n\n  /** ID of the page being read from / written to disk. */\n  page_id_t page_id_;\n\n  /** Callback used to signal to the request issuer when the request has been completed. */\n  std::promise<bool> callback_;\n};\n\n/**\n * @brief The DiskScheduler schedules disk read and write operations.\n *\n * A request is scheduled by calling DiskScheduler::Schedule() with an appropriate DiskRequest object. The scheduler\n * maintains a background worker thread that processes the scheduled requests using the disk manager. The background\n * thread is created in the DiskScheduler constructor and joined in its destructor.\n */\nclass DiskScheduler {\n public:\n  explicit DiskScheduler(DiskManager *disk_manager);\n  ~DiskScheduler();\n\n  void Schedule(std::vector<DiskRequest> &requests);\n\n  void StartWorkerThread();\n\n  using DiskSchedulerPromise = std::promise<bool>;\n\n  /**\n   * @brief Create a Promise object. If you want to implement your own version of promise, you can change this function\n   * so that our test cases can use your promise implementation.\n   *\n   * @return std::promise<bool>\n   */\n  auto CreatePromise() -> DiskSchedulerPromise { return {}; };\n\n  /**\n   * @brief Deallocates a page on disk.\n   *\n   * Note: You should look at the documentation for `DeletePage` in `BufferPoolManager` before using this method.\n   *\n   * @param page_id The page ID of the page to deallocate from disk.\n   */\n  void DeallocatePage(page_id_t page_id) { disk_manager_->DeletePage(page_id); }\n\n private:\n  /** Pointer to the disk manager. */\n  DiskManager *disk_manager_ __attribute__((__unused__));\n  /** A shared queue to concurrently schedule and process requests. When the DiskScheduler's destructor is called,\n   * `std::nullopt` is put into the queue to signal to the background thread to stop execution. */\n  Channel<std::optional<DiskRequest>> request_queue_;\n  /** The background thread responsible for issuing scheduled requests to the disk manager. */\n  std::optional<std::thread> background_thread_;\n};\n}  // namespace bustub\n",
    "filePath": "/workspace/bustub/src/include/storage/disk/disk_scheduler.h",
    "numLines": 91,
    "startLine": 1,
    "totalLines": 91
  },
  "type": "text"
}
